channel.title: Transcripts: Prof G Pod (Scott Galloway)
channel.description: YouTube video transcripts from Prof G Pod (Scott Galloway)

item.title: Hormuz CONFUSION GROWS — Trump DIGS IN on BLOCKADE as Iran CALLS THE SHOTS
item.description:
Trump is claiming a win after the Strait of Hormuz reopens — but the reality is far more complicated.
Jessica Tarlov and Aaron Parnas break down the fragile Middle East ceasefire and the mixed signals coming out of the Trump administration. With reports of backchannel talks, a possible cash-for-uranium proposal, and Trump publicly denying it, the key question at the center of it all: is there a real deal with Iran, or just the illusion of one?
Meanwhile, new concerns are emerging about conditions for U.S. troops in the region, as reports of food shortages raise serious questions about resources and preparation. Back home, Trump is still trying to sell his economic message — while brushing off rising gas prices as “fake,” even as Americans feel the squeeze. Is the White House spin landing, or are voters starting to tune it out?
Plus, RFK Jr. seems to soften his stance on vaccines during tense congressional hearings, and Trump sparks backlash with his latest comments on Epstein, and New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani’s push to tax ultra-wealthy second-home owners gets a noteworthy announcement.
#trump #uspolitics #politics #scottgalloway #jessicatarlov #republicans #gop #republicanparty #democrats #democraticparty #aaronparnas #straitofhormuz #iran #iranwar #blockade #petehegseth #ussoldiers #ceasefire #epstein #jeffepstein #jeffreyepstein #epsteinsurvivors #rfkjr #maha #vaccines #scottbessent #affordability #cornerstores #bodegas #nyc #newyorkcity #zohran #zohranmamdani #taxtherich
Follow Jessica Tarlov, @JessicaTarlov Follow Prof G, @profgalloway Follow Raging Moderates, @RagingModeratesPod
Subscribe to our YouTube Channel: https://www.youtube.com/@RagingModerates
item.content:encoded:
YouTube: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=HPVs8SC5UhA

Transcript

Welcome to Raiku Moderates. I'm Jessica Tarlov. And I'm Aaron Parnes. >> Happy belated birthday. >> Thank you. So exciting. >> Did you take a break from doing the breaking news? >> No, I actually the one thing I wanted for my birthday with the newborn now is to have like a normal day. That's like not newborn heavy. So, I actually worked. I went to the gym. I like felt like a human again. It was really nice. >> Oh, that is nice. You were like, I not going to be with my baby. I'm just going to be with my phone. >> Correct. I'm going to be with myself. And I needed that. But it's good. >> Good. Um I'm glad you found yourself

again. >> Thank you. >> Welcome to 27. It was so long ago. I can't remember what it was like, but I'm sure that I had a good time. >> I'm aging. It's very It's very scary. But here we are. >> Get out of here. All right, let's talk about the war in Iran. The street of Hermuz is back open to commercial traffic as of this morning. That's according to both President Trump and Iran. And it's expected to stay open through the 10day ceasefire. are now in place between Israel and Lebanon. Trump even thanked Iran for reopening the straight, but at the same time made it clear that the US isn't backing off pressure. The blockade on Iranian ports is still in effect until there's a

broader deal. Notably, Trump also referred to it as the Strait of Iran. He loves to rename things. I'm not sure what the meaning is, but that's what we call it now, I guess. At the same time, there's a new political wrinkle back home. House Judiciary Democrats are launching an investigation into Jared Kushner. Congressman Jamie Raskin is raising concern about Kushner's financial ties in the Middle East, especially given his role as a peace envoy. Um, I want to get your take first before I talk about my level of confusion as to what's going on now. But, you know, all eyes on Sunday,

that's when the peace talks should resume in Islam Abad. Um, what do you make of the news of the day that the straight is open and that Trump has said there will be no money exchanged even though there are reports that there will be up to $20 billion exchanged for their highlyenriched uranium. >> He's giving Iran pallets of cash. I mean, >> more pallets than Obama even dreamed of. Do you think that's true though? Because he does know what a salient criticism that was of the JCPOA. I mean, that was the bedrock criticism that we just

handed terrorists, even though it was their money was 1.7 billion. It was frozen and being held in a Qatari bank, >> but they used that to bludgeon the Democrats with. >> Well, for sure. But I mean, I think he understands this time around. You can't get a deal unless Iran gets sanctions relief. I mean, that's what Iran's ultimate bottom line is. It needs money. Oil is how it gets money. It has a ton of sanctioned oil that's frozen. It has a ton of sanctioned funds that are frozen. It's not going to agree to any deal unless you give it money. And so he's going to give Iran pallets of cash. Now, I will say the straight of Hormuz

isn't open. I mean, Iran claims it's open, right? But they have their own conditions. It's open to only commercial vessels that it deems are commercial >> and that they escort them, right? >> Correct. The IRGC is personally escorting them and they're paying tolls. Okay, that was my question because that has been missing from all of the reporting and that was the lynch pin of all of this that they were managing to make either, you know, 2 million per vessel and split that with the Omanis or the money going into a crypto account on a per barrel basis. So, we have no clarity on how much the toll actually

is, just that there is a toll. >> Yeah, Iran is saying that there's still a toll. So, it's not really open. And then you can't claim it's open on Trump's end and then still have a blockade on Iranian ships. That's not open. That's open but right like that's a contingent contingent opening. So I don't think it's open. I I will say this though. Trump is giving Iran everything it wants in a way. Calling this the straight of Iran that gives Iran ter like control of the straight of moose implicitly. Iran didn't have full control of the straight before the war began. Now it will at least based on what these talks are. Um I don't know.

Trump to me is just like ranting on Truth Social doing like mission accomplished every two minutes of like oh my god look at this great thing and look at this great thing but at the end of the day like no one knows what the hell he's talking about. I don't know. >> Okay. Do you I want to get back to the JCPOA for a second because if there is a financial aspect to this right the easing of the sanctions returning the cash and there are sunset clauses on when they can start doing highlyenriched uranium again. That sounds a lot like what the JCPOA was and if it had stayed

in place, we would hopefully be renegotiating the next phase of it basically around this time. Do you think that it is just going to be JCPOA 2026 version or do you think that there is a chance that it's going to be marketkedly different? >> I don't think it'll be marketkedly different. I just don't think it'll be marketkedly better. So, I do think it'll be JCPOA kind of 2.0 and that he'll just say this is his nuclear deal and that it was better than Obama's for whatever reason he's going to say it is. I do

think though that like the United States has less leverage at this point than we did back 10 years ago and also than we did back even six weeks ago because I think what the Iranians have learned and I've said this I think last week on our episode as well is that Iran may not have a nuclear weapon in the conventional sense but the straight of moose is a nuclear weapon and if Iran shuts it down which it did it completely not only tanks the global economy but also tanks Trump's standing among the American population and so Trump knows that very well and right now in my opinion Iran has more leverage than the United States does when it comes to this

negotiation. Because I don't think Iran at the end of the day really cares how many installations the United States bombs as long as it's putting up a lot of pressure and pain on the American people in terms of gas prices, fuel prices, and more. Right. So, >> well, there was also the element that one of the things that has happened since Trump 1.0 is that Iran has become increasingly isolated in their own region. So, you know, you would never think of a scenario in which Pre-Trump, Iran would be hitting Saudi, right, or going after desalination plants or oil

fields or whatever they want to do. But because the Gulf countries are aligned with us now and not with them, you kind of have a madman in the center of the action >> with the control of the straight of Iran to boot. Uh, which makes them a lot more of a loose cannon than I think they would have been necessarily in the past. There's also the Chinese element. The administration is saying, you know, we tal we talked to China, they're not helping at all. Um, but there's been reporting first to the FT, it's been confirmed by several other outlets now, including Jennifer Griffin from Fox,

that the Iranians have been using Chinese satellites to spy on us. And I'm not sure if that is included in the China isn't helping them anymore because I think that was visav actually delivering weapons that they could use um versus their technology. Um and obviously that's a very big deal if it is continuing. >> Oh, for sure. And I think what we're seeing is that um the United States is now somewhat isolating itself on the world stage at the same time. >> What do you make though? I mean I saw there was a joint statement from the UK,

from Germany, France and Italy that they are going to send a naval mission to the straight of Hormuz. So they're using the the old name um to protect freedom of navigation. And it does feel like they were waiting until this moment. Trump's out there saying like NATO, you know, 20 minutes too late. You're just a paper tiger. Um, but it feels like they are inching towards participation. >> Yeah. I mean, so I I think Europe is realizing that it needs to be a leader in its own right. I think that's what we're seeing more so than Europe kind of

capitulating to Trump. I think Europe is realizing, okay, the United States isn't going to be there for us whenever we need the United States. Obviously, Russia's a threat. The Iranians um have been a threat. The Chinese are what they are. So, we need to be our own kind of leadership block. And I think that's what we're seeing. And I also think over the next many years in the second Trump term, Europe is going to only have to step up more to defend itself and to protect its own interests as America pulls back. So, I don't necessarily think this is because of Trump. More so, I think this is because Europe needs the

oil and Europe is trying to get it for themselves. Oh, I mean that does make sense, too. And it kind of kills two birds with one stone, right? That you can say to Trump like, "We're helping out, but really it's about the fact that Europe's going to run out of jet fuel in 6 weeks." >> Correct. >> I was talking to a friend that flies all the time and says that Lufansza hasn't flown in 3 days. They're saying that it's a pilot strike, but apparently in history they've only ever gone one day without flying before. So, obviously, it's hitting them very hard. Um, I wanted to before we move topic, talk a little bit about morale within the

troops. There's this photo of the very meager meals that troops are receiving that apparently have been photo snapped and sent home to family members. Do you get a sense of how folks within the armed forces are feeling about this war and kind of what it portends also for support for the administration? I've spoken to a lot of service members, uh, specifically family members of service members who have been deployed, and they tell me the same thing as like that USA Today article. It's just morale is at an

all-time low, right? Because a lot of these service members, these aircraft carrier groups are being deployed for longer than they should be. And as a result, you have things like toilets being clogged, not enough toilets for um, shipment on a aircraft carrier. You have uh, lack of provisions. you right now they can't a lot of families are worried because they can't send anything to the Middle East typically when you have a son or a daughter deployed you can send them a care package right now that's been suspended indefinitely because of the war so even if they wanted to send their daughter for example tampons which they're running

out of on some of these ships they can't do that and it and it's a lot of people are frustrated right you have America spending over a billion dollars a year in defense spending or sorry more than a trillion dollars next year more than a trillion and a half potentially if Congress goes along with Trump's plan and our service members are eating um brown meat, mystery meat, can't get tampons, can't get hygiene products. I mean, it's unacceptable. It >> it definitely is. I' I've seen some reports that there's going to be push back even from the GOP on the $ 1.5 trillion spending bill for next year. Um

but I imagine that there are ways around it that President Trump and his allies will get to. Um, but it's really sad and it's great that you've become a a point of contact for people uh to talk about these kinds of things. I mean, just to get on my lady high horse for a second, you always hear about women's sanitary products being the first things to go in this or that even like women's shelters, domestic violence shelters, etc. don't have them, >> right? >> The pink tax is real. Um, I could do a whole other episode about that, but >> it's it's real 100%. girl, dad. Got to

be thinking about those things. >> I I will say in law school, um the one thing I did in student government that I ushered through was making sure that there were um feminine products in every bathroom across the school for free. That was like my one thing that I did. So, um I've been dealing with this for a while. >> I love that. That's a important one thing to do. I want to talk a little bit about what's going on at home. You know, we have this war going on and then they're still trying to sell the big beautiful bill and the economic policies. It was tax day this week. Uh average American got a $375 refund. Um

you will be paying $740 more at the pump, which doesn't seem like a great tradeoff. Uh we had Door Dash Grandma and at an event in Las Vegas, uh Trump waved off rising gas prices as fake inflation despite fuel costs spiking and travel getting more expensive. The message is basically don't believe what you're paying. I would say not to call out my own kind, but we tried that and it really failed. Uh so I don't know why they're repeating our mistakes. All of this comes as younger voters grow more uneasy about the economy. And as Trump heads to Arizona today to rally support

at a Turning Point USA event with Republicans, they are worried about the chaos drowning out their message. Here's Trump this week not quite understanding what a corner store is. millions of American small businesses, including restaurants, strike laners, corner stores. What is a corner store? I've never heard that term. I know what a corner store is, but I've never heard it described. A corner store. Who the hell wrote that, please? And more. >> He knows what it is, but he doesn't know what the hell that it is. And who wrote that? It's also the most obvious thing,

right? It's the store on the corner. Well, I mean, you you wouldn't expect someone who grew up in New York and was like this like made man in New York City walking the streets of New York, he wouldn't know what a quarter store is. So, >> if we had had said bodega, maybe he would have >> I actually wish they wrote him bodega because I think you'd get a Joe Biden 2.0 moment and he'd mispronounce bodega. >> What do you think is going on here, though? So, I mean, he went a little off script about the Door Dash grandma, admitting that it was a tacky stunt. I also don't know how we're not discussing

the fact that the problem is that a grandma has to be doing Door Dash, >> right? And this grandma who has been a prop at multiple GOP events, we should also add to that. But like that seems like the problem that a nice old lady from Arkansas whose husband has cancer cannot be at home with him as a caregiver and has to deliver McDonald's >> to the Oval Office. Like that is the problem with America. And seniors are the ones who are even getting the most out of the big beautiful bill besides the mega rich, right? because of the 6K refund on social security which does

sunset out and the no tax on social security is also a lie. We go down the list of it but >> but she got a $100 tip. >> She got a tip. >> I I just I hate everything sometimes. Let's take a quick break. Stay with us. Welcome back. What's going on with younger people? there is no group aside from Latino voters where Trump's favorability has fallen the fastest. Um, what do you think their thinking about this like when they see

stuff like this? Cuz his stunts have resonated before like the >> McDonald's during the campaign, the garbage truck, etc. You know, it was like Trump is so funny and it seems like they don't find him so funny anymore. Well, it's different when you're campaigning and saying you're going to do all these things and then you're in office and have the power to do all these things and you're not actually doing them. So, I think that's the difference, right? Like he campaigned unaffordability. America is more unaffordable today than it was when he took office. And young people see that we go to the gas pumps. We're paying $700 more in gas, right? my

alma moater GW $100,000 a year for tuition which I don't know that >> which I don't know what we're paying $100,000 for and I'm not giving money back to my school like they don't need the money they're charging people up the wazoo right so like America is unaffordable it's less affordable today than it was a year ago it's less affordable today than it was 2 years ago and young people are fed up and it's again a a president a politician who sold them this bill of goods and they're not actually getting anything good out of it. So, I don't

know what Trump expects. I mean, I don't think he cares personally because he's like, "Well, I'm leaving. I'm not running for re-election no matter how much he wants to." But, I don't think he realizes and and I really hope that the Democrats kind of follow through on their threats. What these investigations are going to be like for the next two years when they take over the House and the Senate because it's going to be a painful two years for him. I don't think he realizes that. >> I I don't know. those Anthony Scaramucci came on and he was saying something similar that Trump doesn't really care what happens after him because at least

he can then also say like you couldn't do it without me basically but living through the last two years of his administration if Democrats I mean we should reclaim the house but potentially you know taking the Senate back is going to be a very ugly process. Um I want to talk Scott Bessant for a second. Um, another report that he beat up another member of the administration. I just love how many people he punches. It was Bill Py. There's also reporting that Py was the one behind the AI generated image of him as Jesus that he then said

was a doctor. >> Oh, really? >> Yeah. Which I kind of loved. I was like, all roads lead to Bill Py being awful. Okay. So, Bessant maybe punches Bill Py. And then he also had to say this about affordability which really blew my mind and I was bummed. I couldn't work it in on the five. >> Why aren't we seeing this sentiment? Why aren't people feeling better? >> Well, I you know, I I when I was in the investment business, there are consumer uh surveys, but I used to look at what are the people really doing. So at Treasury we have loads of CEOs come

through whether they're in retail, credit card, the banks and the consumer uh while they may be uh sounding uh grim is actually quite buoyant. >> So we we are seeing spending has been very solid across most categories. >> So they might not feel good but >> well look in their heart of hearts they feel good. I'm I'm not sure what they're telling the survey people. Can someone like ask him how much he pays for a gallon of milk, please? Cuz I don't think he even knows.

>> In his Barbie dreamhouse, >> he's worth $500 million. Like, it doesn't impact him. This is >> chunky. >> The new Wilbur Ross with the Campbell's soup can. It's also crazy as the consumer sentiment data shows the lowest in recorded history. Like, they just started in the 1960s, so we couldn't even go further back than that. It has never felt as shitty for Americans as it does right now. And he's like, "Well, actually, if you look inside your heart, you'll know how good you have it." >> I look inside my heart every day. I

don't feel like I'm paying less today and it's great in the economy and all that jazz. So, >> well, also nothing more expensive than a newborn baby. >> Correct. Everything is insane. I mean, talk about a >> such a racket. >> Yeah, >> I know. Yeah. Especially the diapers and they go through them so fast. >> I I think diapers should be free. Um, but that's my other hot take is that Congress should pass legislation to subsidize all diaper purchases. >> I love it. I I can't get Hugim Jeff to give us any policies, but I we could run

an entire midterm on free diapers. Yeah, let's both hit them with that. Uh, free diapers would be great. I want to talk a little RFK Jr. Uh, he's back on the hill for day two. He's going to go 7 days, which feels like a slow and painful death. Um, he's already talked about issues of fraud and vaccines, other public health issues. This is also the sidebyside on the raccoon penis story, which we did cover on the five. I couldn't get Besson in, but we talked about the raccoon. But in a seeming reversal, Kennedy testified that the

measles vaccine was safe and effective for most people and agreed it was safer than contracting measles. So, you know, progress is slow but certain. This comes after President Trump announced he'll nominate Dr. Erica Schwarz to head the CDC, a choice who has in the past shown support for vaccines and appears to be a normal human being. My feeling on this is that the administration knows what a liability RFK Jr. is. And so they're bringing in someone that can be a more competent face to the health and safety side of this and let him just talk about

red dyes and yellow dyes in food and things like that that are safer for all of us. But uh what's your RFK read? >> No, I I actually agree with you on that front because I I was looking into her and I thought that she was >> a normie. Like I mean she was like okay like fine, wellqualified. She'll probably be confirmed overwhelmingly. Like I don't think unless they dig up something in her past that I couldn't find. RFK Jr. I feel like what they do with these um cabinet secretaries when they don't like them they put them in front of Congress and they're just like let's see how you do. They did that with Pam Bondi

>> like the Hunger Games. >> No, but literally Pam Bondi down under 50,000. She's out a couple months later. Christy Gnome lies in front of Congress and John Kennedy out a few days later. Could RFK Jr. be next? Potentially. Potentially. >> Oh, I like it. Also, Could I put in a request? I would like to see Pete Hexth on the Hill. I would like more Tulsa. >> Well, then you're going to have to work with him again. >> I don't think that. Don't talk to me about that. I don't think I don't know how the exodus works. Like if you leave the administration, if you just

>> if you go back >> if you come back, the seats have been filled. Want to do a little Epstein as well with you. We had the weird Melania Trump statement. Um, and now seems like that's linked to the story that Amanda Angaro is going to tell. What is your read on kind of where that stands? And President Trump has apparently said that he's okay with the survivors having a public hearing. Do you believe that? And what do you think is coming on the Melania front? >> Of course, I believe that because he's

setting them up for failure. I thought Melania was setting them up for failure, too. I mean, and the survivors said this themselves, it's not their responsibility to testify in front of Congress. It's their responsibility to give information to law enforcement, which they did, right? Like law enforcement hasn't done the job. So, what I've been saying, what I've been screaming for like at the top of my lungs for the past week is that this is just Todd Blanch is newly installed as a acting attorney general. They want to throw the heat off of the Department of Justice as it relates to Epstein. And so, what are they going to do? Oh, Congress, it's your responsibility now. Hold a hearing. And if you don't hold a

hearing, then Blanch will say, "Well, we gave them the opportunity and they didn't hold a hearing." That's all that's happening here. Um, they're setting the survivors up for failure. And I mean, Trump says victims or whatever. I mean, he a derogatory remark about the survivors. And as far as Melania Trump thing goes, I think that she's been personally peeved about um, everyone talking about her connection, a valid connection to Glenn Maxwell and Jeffrey Epstein. You have an email, very friendly email, mind you, >> um, where where she wants to talk with Maxwell on the phone. I think she's upset that people keep talking about it and she just wants to do her own thing and she wants to get out in front of

something that's coming out. Could be the Amanda Angaro thing. It could be something regarding Michael Wolf. It could be something regarding Pam Bondi. I don't know. I don't think anyone really knows right now. Or it could just be Melania just being Melania and just saying like, you know, it's screw it. I'm going to come out and say my piece and move on cuz no one wants to hear it inside the administration. I don't know. >> Yeah. I mean, I'm not even sure actually that we'll really find out. Uh, that's a good point about the survivors and that's also coupled with Todd Blanch went on Fox earlier this week and said that all the documents were out. >> Yeah. >> Like Dana and Bill Hemer asked him and he was like, "Nope, we did our job."

>> Correct. >> If they put Todd Blanch up, not just as interim AG, but for confirmation, do you think he gets confirmed? >> I do because you only need 50 votes. Um, >> yeah. I But Tom Tillis still exists even though Trump told Maria Barto Romo he wasn't a senator anymore. But but Tom Telis exists to a point. He'll still confirm he he voted to confirm Blanch the first time around. Like nothing's changed since Blanch was first confirmed. Like we we knew this who this is who he was. And they're all going to come back to oh the president deserves to have whatever he wants in the cabinet. Blah blah blah blah.

>> Well, especially if the Democrats are about to come into power and make our lives miserable, so we better do things fast. Correct. Which brings me to a question that I have wanted to ask you and meant to text you about. >> Justice Ted Cruz. >> Yes. Justice Ted Cruz or based Mike Lee, which I I think is a complete I think all of that is a complete joke. But do you think that they are going to force Alo out in time, try to get another nominee through before the midterms? >> I do, but I actually don't know why they would. I I I have such a hard time with

this. If I'm a Donald Trump, the one justice who's always on my side and is so radical to the point that like he'll do anything I ask him to do is Alito. Alto finds a way to literally agree to with everything this administration does, even if it is a radical position. I don't for I don't want to force someone like that out. I I just don't I think Alto can survive another six years if he has to. Now, I don't know that he wants to. I think I I think he's going to retire in July, but I don't know that if I'm Trump, I want him out. But then again, like the alternative, it could be someone like Ted Cruz. I wouldn't put it

past Trump to nominate Ted Cruz to the to the Supreme Court. I don't know that he'd get confirmed, but >> that feels like a bridge too far and that also the senators know too much. >> Yeah. >> They've been dealing with him forever. >> Sure. I would argue Kavanaaugh was a bridge too far though, right? Like the allegations, right? Like that to me, >> but they didn't know him. I mean, it it is well documented that even Ted Cruz's friends don't like Ted Cruz. Sure. >> So, he has also been much more biased. >> Or what about Justice Eileen Cannon,

right? Like I wouldn't put that past Trump. >> Oh, our old friend from the Mara Lago documents case in Jan 6th. >> Exactly. >> She she has been working overtime. I want to wrap on Mom Donnie. Um we had his, you know, first 100 days. He has a new policy for taxing the rich, appeals worth over 5 million that aren't the owner's primary residence. Um, it's supported by 93% of New Yorkers. No one asked me. Fake polls. I don't know. But actually, this makes a lot of sense. >> Doesn't apply to you either.

>> It definitely does not apply to me. Um, but he had a bit of fun with it at the press conference. Let's look at that. Philanthropy has long played an essential role in New York City as an incubator for change and as a powerful tool to expand access to city programs. I like to think of it this way. Government is driving the race car and philanthropy is there to give it that turbo boost to cross the finish line. Or if you are a Mario Kart fan, government is Yoshi and philanthropy is the golden mushroom, that edge we need to beat

Bowser on the Rainbow Road. To belabor this metaphor even further, Bowser is corporate greed in this scenario. I I love young people in office. Like, come on. >> I was about to say he's so millennial coded, which is like old for you, but still young. >> Definitely old for me, but still young. I mean, I feel old these days. I'm 27, so I mean, I'm almost there. But yeah, I like go Zoron. I think, you know, he's winning a lot of people over who were not enthusiastic about his mayoralty.

And you're seeing that not only in the surveys, but just in real life. Like I spoke at an event on Monday night with a bunch of centrist Democrats. Um, a lot of Jews who were very concerned about what was going to happen under Mom Donniey's control in the city. And I was talking about how people are really connecting with the fact that it's just obvious that mom Donnie cares and how desperate people had been for folks in elected office who seemed like they cared about the place that they represented but especially cared about the most vulnerable. And no one can

really fight with that. You can say I you know the free grocery store that will cost $30 million and won't be out for a few years. You can pick at that stuff, of course, but >> No, I think he's doing I think he's doing great work. And honestly, someone else I think that's doing great work that needs to be highlighted, Jessica Tarlov has a new book out. Go pre-order it right now. >> Um, go on. That's enough. >> September 15th, it's out, but go buy it now. Yeah, >> totally. I'm excited about it. >> Me, too. >> As a reminder, Raging Moderates is 5 days a week. New episodes dropping every weekday evening on YouTube, Apple

Podcast, Spotify, everywhere else. Also, in addition to being five days a week, we're now on Substack. Subscribers get ad free episodes, live streams, place to engage with me and Scott, other listeners, the whole ProfG fam. Find us at raging moderates.profia.com. Aaron, it was great to see you and I'll see you next week. >> See you next week. >> Bye everyone.
